title: Paradise Lost - Book VI
content:
آ آ آ آ All night the dreadless Angel unpursuï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Through Heavï؟½ns wide Champain held his way, till Morn,
آ آ آ آ Wakï؟½t by the circling Hours, with rosie hand
آ آ آ آ Unbarrï؟½d the gates of Light. There is a Cave
آ آ آ آ Within the Mount of God, fast by his Throne,
آ آ آ آ Where light and darkness in perpetual round
آ آ آ آ Lodge and dislodge by turns, which makes through Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Grateful vicissitude, like Day and Night;
آ آ آ آ Light issues forth, and at the other dore
آ آ آ آ Obsequious darkness enters, till her houre
آ آ آ آ To veile the Heavï؟½n, though darkness there might well
آ آ آ آ Seem twilight here; and now went forth the Morn
آ آ آ آ Such as in highest Heavï؟½n, arrayd in Gold
آ آ آ آ Empyreal, from before her vanisht Night,
آ آ آ آ Shot through with orient Beams: when all the Plain
آ آ آ آ Coverd with thick embatteld Squadrons bright,
آ آ آ آ Chariots and flaming Armes, and fierie Steeds
آ آ آ آ Reflecting blaze on blaze, first met his view:
آ آ آ آ Warr he perceavï؟½d, warr in procinct, and found
آ آ آ آ Already known what he for news had thought
آ آ آ آ To have reported: gladly then he mixt
آ آ آ آ Among those friendly Powers who him receavï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With joy and acclamations loud, that one
آ آ آ آ That of so many Myriads fallï؟½n, yet one
آ آ آ آ Returnd not lost: On to the sacred hill
آ آ آ آ They led him high applauded, and present
آ آ آ آ Before the seat supream; from whence a voice
آ آ آ آ From midst a Golden Cloud thus milde was heard.
آ آ آ آ Servant of God, well done, well hast thou fought
آ آ آ آ The better fight, who single hast maintaind
آ آ آ آ Against revolted multitudes the Cause
آ آ آ آ Of Truth, in word mightier then they in Armes;
آ آ آ آ And for the testimonie of Truth hast born
آ آ آ آ Universal reproach, far worse to beare
آ آ آ آ Then violence: for this was all thy care
آ آ آ آ To stand approvï؟½d in sight of God, though Worlds
آ آ آ آ Judgï؟½d thee perverse: the easier conquest now
آ آ آ آ Remains thee, aided by this host of friends,
آ آ آ آ Back on thy foes more glorious to return
آ آ آ آ Then scornd thou didst depart, and to subdue
آ آ آ آ By force, who reason for thir Law refuse,
آ آ آ آ Right reason for thir Law, and for thir King
آ آ آ آ Messiah, who by right of merit Reigns.
آ آ آ آ Go Michael of Celestial Armies Prince,
آ آ آ آ And thou in Military prowess next
آ آ آ آ Gabriel, lead forth to Battel these my Sons
آ آ آ آ Invincible, lead forth my armed Saints
آ آ آ آ By Thousands and by Millions rangï؟½d for fight;
آ آ آ آ Equal in number to that Godless crew
آ آ آ آ Rebellious, them with Fire and hostile Arms
آ آ آ آ Fearless assault, and to the brow of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Pursuing drive them out from God and bliss,
آ آ آ آ Into thir place of punishment, the Gulf
آ آ آ آ Of Tartarus, which ready opens wide
آ آ آ آ His fiery Chaos to receave thir fall.
آ آ آ آ So spake the Sovran voice, and Clouds began
آ آ آ آ To darken all the Hill, and smoak to rowl
آ آ آ آ In duskie wreathes, reluctant flames, the signe
آ آ آ آ Of wrauth awakï؟½t: nor with less dread the loud
آ آ آ آ Ethereal Trumpet from on high gan blow:
آ آ آ آ At which command the Powers Militant,
آ آ آ آ That stood for Heavï؟½n, in mighty Quadrate joynï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Of Union irresistible, movï؟½d on
آ آ آ آ In silence thir bright Legions, to the sound
آ آ آ آ Of instrumental Harmonie that breathï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Heroic Ardor to adventï؟½rous deeds
آ آ آ آ Under thir God-like Leaders, in the Cause
آ آ آ آ Of God and his Messiah. On they move
آ آ آ آ Indissolubly firm; nor obvious Hill,
آ آ آ آ Nor streitï؟½ning Vale, nor Wood, nor Stream divides
آ آ آ آ Thir perfet ranks; for high above the ground
آ آ آ آ Thir march was, and the passive Air upbore
آ آ آ آ Thir nimble tread; as when the total kind
آ آ آ آ Of Birds in orderly array on wing
آ آ آ آ Came summond over Eden to receive
آ آ آ آ Thir names of thee; so over many a tract
آ آ آ آ Of Heavï؟½n they marchï؟½d, and many a Province wide
آ آ آ آ Tenfold the length of this terrene: at last
آ آ آ آ Farr in thï؟½ Horizon to the North appeerï؟½d
آ آ آ آ From skirt to skirt a fierie Region, stretcht
آ آ آ آ In battailous aspect, and neerer view
آ آ آ آ Bristlï؟½d with upright beams innumerable
آ آ آ آ Of rigid Spears, and Helmets throngï؟½d, and Shields
آ آ آ آ Various, with boastful Argument portraid,
آ آ آ آ The banded Powers of Satan hasting on
آ آ آ آ With furious expedition; for they weend
آ آ آ آ That self same day by fight, or by surprize
آ آ آ آ To win the Mount of God, and on his Throne
آ آ آ آ To set the envier of his State, the proud
آ آ آ آ Aspirer, but thir thoughts provï؟½d fond and vain
آ آ آ آ In the mid way: though strange to us it seemd
آ آ آ آ At first, that Angel should with Angel warr,
آ آ آ آ And in fierce hosting meet, who wont to meet
آ آ آ آ So oft in Festivals of joy and love
آ آ آ آ Unanimous, as sons of one great Sire
آ آ آ آ Hymning thï؟½ Eternal Father: but the shout
آ آ آ آ Of Battel now began, and rushing sound
آ آ آ آ Of onset ended soon each milder thought.
آ آ آ آ High in the midst exalted as a God
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Apostat in his Sun-bright Chariot sate
آ آ آ آ Idol of Majestie Divine, enclosï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With Flaming Cherubim, and golden Shields;
آ آ آ آ Then lighted from his gorgeous Throne, for now
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt Host and Host but narrow space was left,
آ آ آ آ A dreadful interval, and Front to Front
آ آ آ آ Presented stood in terrible array
آ آ آ آ Of hideous length: before the cloudie Van,
آ آ آ آ On the rough edge of battel ere it joynï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Satan with vast and haughtie strides advancï؟½t,
آ آ آ آ Came towring, armd in Adamant and Gold;
آ آ آ آ Abdiel that sight endurï؟½d not, where he stood
آ آ آ آ Among the mightiest, bent on highest deeds,
آ آ آ آ And thus his own undaunted heart explores.
آ آ آ آ O Heavï؟½n! that such resemblance of the Highest
آ آ آ آ Should yet remain, where faith and realtie
آ آ آ آ Remain not; wherfore should not strength and might
آ آ آ آ There fail where Vertue fails, or weakest prove
آ آ آ آ Where boldest; though to sight unconquerable?
آ آ آ آ His puissance, trusting in thï؟½ Almightieï؟½s aide,
آ آ آ آ I mean to try, whose Reason I have triï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Unsound and false; nor is it aught but just,
آ آ آ آ That he who in debate of Truth hath won,
آ آ آ آ Should win in Arms, in both disputes alike
آ آ آ آ Victor; though brutish that contest and foule,
آ آ آ آ When Reason hath to deal with force, yet so
آ آ آ آ Most reason is that Reason overcome.
آ آ آ آ So pondering, and from his armed Peers
آ آ آ آ Forth stepping opposite, half way he met
آ آ آ آ His daring foe, at this prevention more
آ آ آ آ Incensï؟½t, and thus securely him defiï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Proud, art thou met? thy hope was to have reacht
آ آ آ آ The highth of thy aspiring unopposï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ The Throne of God unguarded, and his side
آ آ آ آ Abandond at the terror of thy Power
آ آ آ آ Or potent tongue; fool, not to think how vain
آ آ آ آ Against thï؟½ Omnipotent to rise in Arms;
آ آ آ آ Who out of smallest things could without end
آ آ آ آ Have raisï؟½d incessant Armies to defeat
آ آ آ آ Thy folly; or with solitarie hand
آ آ آ آ Reaching beyond all limit, at one blow
آ آ آ آ Unaided could have finisht thee, and whelmd
آ آ آ آ Thy Legions under darkness; but thou seest
آ آ آ آ All are not of thy Train; there be who Faith
آ آ آ آ Prefer, and Pietie to God, though then
آ آ آ آ To thee not visible, when I alone
آ آ آ آ Seemd in thy World erroneous to dissent
آ آ آ آ From all: my Sect thou seest, now learn too late
آ آ آ آ How few somtimes may know, when thousands err.
آ آ آ آ Whom the grand foe with scornful eye askance
آ آ آ آ Thus answerd. Ill for thee, but in wisht houre
آ آ آ آ Of my revenge, first sought for thou returnst
آ آ آ آ From flight, seditious Angel, to receave
آ آ آ آ Thy merited reward, the first assay
آ آ آ آ Of this right hand provokï؟½t, since first that tongue
آ آ آ آ Inspirï؟½d with contradiction durst oppose
آ آ آ آ A third part of the Gods, in Synod met
آ آ آ آ Thir Deities to assert, who while they feel
آ آ آ آ Vigour Divine within them, can allow
آ آ آ آ Omnipotence to none. But well thou comst
آ آ آ آ Before thy fellows, ambitious to win
آ آ آ آ From me som Plume, that thy success may show
آ آ آ آ Destruction to the rest: this pause between
آ آ آ آ (Unanswerd least thou boast) to let thee know;
آ آ آ آ At first I thought that Libertie and Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ To heavï؟½nly Soules had bin all one; but now
آ آ آ آ I see that most through sloth had rather serve,
آ آ آ آ Ministring Spirits, traind up in Feast and Song;
آ آ آ آ Such hast thou armï؟½d, the Minstrelsie of Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ Servilitie with freedom to contend,
آ آ آ آ As both thir deeds comparï؟½d this day shall prove.
آ آ آ آ To whom in brief thus Abdiel stern repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Apostat, still thou errst, nor end wilt find
آ آ آ آ Of erring, from the path of truth remote:
آ آ آ آ Unjustly thou depravï؟½st it with the name
آ آ آ آ Of Servitude to serve whom God ordains,
آ آ آ آ Or Nature; God and Nature bid the same,
آ آ آ آ When he who rules is worthiest, and excells
آ آ آ آ Them whom he governs. This is servitude,
آ آ آ آ To serve thï؟½ unwise, or him who hath rebelld
آ آ آ آ Against his worthier, as thine now serve thee,
آ آ آ آ Thy self not free, but to thy self enthrallï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Yet leudly darï؟½st our ministring upbraid.
آ آ آ آ Reign thou in Hell thy Kingdom, let mee serve
آ آ آ آ In Heavï؟½n God ever blessed, and his Divine
آ آ آ آ Behests obey, worthiest to be obeyï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Yet Chains in Hell, not Realms expect: mean while
آ آ آ آ From mee returnd, as erst thou saidst, from flight,
آ آ آ آ This greeting on thy impious Crest receive.
آ آ آ آ So saying, a noble stroke he lifted high,
آ آ آ آ Which hung not, but so swift with tempest fell
آ آ آ آ On the proud Crest of Satan, that no sight,
آ آ آ آ Nor motion of swift thought, less could his Shield
آ آ آ آ Such ruin intercept: ten paces huge
آ آ آ آ He back recoild; the tenth on bended knee
آ آ آ آ His massie Spear upstaid; as if on Earth
آ آ آ آ Winds under ground or waters forcing way
آ آ آ آ Sidelong, had pushï؟½t a Mountain from his seat
آ آ آ آ Half sunk with all his Pines. Amazement seisï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The Rebel Thrones, but greater rage to see
آ آ آ آ Thus foilï؟½d thir mightiest, ours joy filld, and shout,
آ آ آ آ Presage of Victorie and fierce desire
آ آ آ آ Of Battel: whereat Michael bid sound
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Arch-Angel trumpet; through the vast of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ It sounded, and the faithful Armies rung
آ آ آ آ Hosanna to the Highest: nor stood at gaze
آ آ آ آ The adverse Legions, nor less hideous joynï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The horrid shock: now storming furie rose,
آ آ آ آ And clamour such as heard in Heavï؟½n till now
آ آ آ آ Was never, Arms on Armour clashing brayï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Horrible discord, and the madding Wheeles
آ آ آ آ Of brazen Chariots ragï؟½d; dire was the noise
آ آ آ آ Of conflict; over head the dismal hiss
آ آ آ آ Of fiery Darts in flaming volies flew,
آ آ آ آ And flying vaulted either Host with fire.
آ آ آ آ So under fierie Cope together rushï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Both Battels maine, with ruinous assault
آ آ آ آ And inextinguishable rage; all Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Resounded, and had Earth bin then, all Earth
آ آ آ آ Had to her Center shook. What wonder? when
آ آ آ آ Millions of fierce encountring Angels fought
آ آ آ آ On either side, the least of whom could weild
آ آ آ آ These Elements, and arm him with the force
آ آ آ آ Of all thir Regions: how much more of Power
آ آ آ آ Armie against Armie numberless to raise
آ آ آ آ Dreadful combustion warring, and disturb,
آ آ آ آ Though not destroy, thir happie Native seat;
آ آ آ آ Had not thï؟½ Eternal King Omnipotent
آ آ آ آ From his strong hold of Heavï؟½n high over-rulï؟½d
آ آ آ آ And limited thir might; though numberd such
آ آ آ آ As each divided Legion might have seemd
آ آ آ آ A numerous Host, in strength each armed hand
آ آ آ آ A Legion; led in fight, yet Leader seemd
آ آ آ آ Each Warriour single as in Chief, expert
آ آ آ آ When to advance, or stand, or turn the sway
آ آ آ آ Of Battel, open when, and when to close
آ آ آ آ The ridges of grim Warr; no thought of flight,
آ آ آ آ None of retreat, no unbecoming deed
آ آ آ آ That arguï؟½d fear; each on himself reliï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ As onely in his arm the moment lay
آ آ آ آ Of victorie; deeds of eternal fame
آ آ آ آ Were don, but infinite: for wide was spred
آ آ آ آ That Warr and various; somtimes on firm ground
آ آ آ آ A standing fight, then soaring on main wing
آ آ آ آ Tormented all the Air; all Air seemd then
آ آ آ آ Conflicting Fire: long time in eeven scale
آ آ آ آ The Battel hung; till Satan, who that day
آ آ آ آ Prodigious power had shewn, and met in Armes
آ آ آ آ No equal, raunging through the dire attack
آ آ آ آ Of fighting Seraphim confusï؟½d, at length
آ آ آ آ Saw where the Sword of Michael smote, and fellï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Squadrons at once, with huge two-handed sway
آ آ آ آ Brandisht aloft the horrid edge came down
آ آ آ آ Wide wasting; such destruction to withstand
آ آ آ آ He hasted, and opposï؟½d the rockie Orb
آ آ آ آ Of tenfold Adamant, his ample Shield
آ آ آ آ A vast circumference: At his approach
آ آ آ آ The great Arch-Angel from his warlike toile
آ آ آ آ Surceasï؟½d, and glad as hoping here to end
آ آ آ آ Intestine War in Heavï؟½n, the arch foe subduï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Or Captive dragï؟½d in Chains, with hostile frown
آ آ آ آ And visage all enflamï؟½d first thus began.
آ آ آ آ Author of evil, unknown till thy revolt,
آ آ آ آ Unnamï؟½d in Heavï؟½n, now plenteous, as thou seest
آ آ آ آ These Acts of hateful strife, hateful to all,
آ آ آ آ Though heaviest by just measure on thy self
آ آ آ آ And thy adherents: how hast thou disturbï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Heavï؟½ns blessed peace, and into Nature brought
آ آ آ آ Miserie, uncreated till the crime
آ آ آ آ Of thy Rebellion? how hast thou instillï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Thy malice into thousands, once upright
آ آ آ آ And faithful, now provï؟½d false. But think not here
آ آ آ آ To trouble Holy Rest; Heavï؟½n casts thee out
آ آ آ آ From all her Confines. Heavï؟½n the seat of bliss
آ آ آ آ Brooks not the works of violence and Warr.
آ آ آ آ Hence then, and evil go with thee along
آ آ آ آ Thy ofspring, to the place of evil, Hell,
آ آ آ آ Thou and thy wicked crew; there mingle broiles,
آ آ آ آ Ere this avenging Sword begin thy doome,
آ آ آ آ Or som more sudden vengeance wingï؟½d from God
آ آ آ آ Precipitate thee with augmented paine.
آ آ آ آ So spake the Prince of Angels; to whom thus
آ آ آ آ The Adversarie. Nor think thou with wind
آ آ آ آ Of airie threats to aw whom yet with deeds
آ آ آ آ Thou canst not. Hast thou turnd the least of these
آ آ آ آ To flight, or if to fall, but that they rise
آ آ آ آ Unvanquisht, easier to transact with mee
آ آ آ آ That thou shouldst hope, imperious, and with threats
آ آ آ آ To chase me hence? erre not that so shall end
آ آ آ آ The strife which thou callï؟½st evil, but wee style
آ آ آ آ The strife of Glorie: which we mean to win,
آ آ آ آ Or turn this Heavï؟½n it self into the Hell
آ آ آ آ Thou fablest, here however to dwell free,
آ آ آ آ If not to reign: mean while thy utmost force,
آ آ آ آ And join him namï؟½d Almightie to thy aid,
آ آ آ آ I flie not, but have sought thee farr and nigh.
آ آ آ آ They ended parle, and both addrest for fight
آ آ آ آ Unspeakable; for who, though with the tongue
آ آ آ آ Of Angels, can relate, or to what things
آ آ آ آ Liken on Earth conspicuous, that may lift
آ آ آ آ Human imagination to such highth
آ آ آ آ Of Godlike Power: for likest Gods they seemd,
آ آ آ آ Stood they or movï؟½d, in stature, motion, arms
آ آ آ آ Fit to decide the Empire of great Heavï؟½n.
آ آ آ آ Now wavï؟½d thir fierie Swords, and in the Aire
آ آ آ آ Made horrid Circles; two broad Suns thir Shields
آ آ آ آ Blazï؟½d opposite, while expectation stood
آ آ آ آ In horror; from each hand with speed retirï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Where erst was thickest fight, thï؟½ Angelic throng,
آ آ آ آ And left large field, unsafe within the wind
آ آ آ آ Of such commotion, such as to set forth
آ آ آ آ Great things by small, If Natures concord broke,
آ آ آ آ Among the Constellations warr were sprung,
آ آ آ آ Two Planets rushing from aspect maligne
آ آ آ آ Of fiercest opposition in mid Skie,
آ آ آ آ Should combat, and thir jarring Sphears confound.
آ آ آ آ Together both with next to Almightie Arme,
آ آ آ آ Uplifted imminent one stroke they aimï؟½d
آ آ آ آ That might determine, and not need repeate,
آ آ آ آ As not of power, at once; nor odds appeerd
آ آ آ آ In might or swift prevention; but the sword
آ آ آ آ Of Michael from the Armorie of God
آ آ آ آ Was givï؟½n him temperd so, that neither keen
آ آ آ آ Nor solid might resist that edge: it met
آ آ آ آ The sword of Satan with steep force to smite
آ آ آ آ Descending, and in half cut sheere, nor staid,
آ آ آ آ But with swift wheele reverse, deep entring sharï؟½d
آ آ آ آ All his right side; then Satan first knew pain,
آ آ آ آ And writhï؟½d him to and fro convolvï؟½d; so sore
آ آ آ آ The griding sword with discontinuous wound
آ آ آ آ Passï؟½d through him, but thï؟½ Ethereal substance closï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Not long divisible, and from the gash
آ آ آ آ A stream of Nectarous humor issuing flowï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Sanguin, such as Celestial Spirits may bleed,
آ آ آ آ And all his Armour staind ere while so bright.
آ آ آ آ Forthwith on all sides to his aide was run
آ آ آ آ By Angels many and strong, who interposï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Defence, while others bore him on thir Shields
آ آ آ آ Back to his Chariot; where it stood retirï؟½d
آ آ آ آ From off the files of warr; there they him laid
آ آ آ آ Gnashing for anguish and despite and shame
آ آ آ آ To find himself not matchless, and his pride
آ آ آ آ Humblï؟½d by such rebuke, so farr beneath
آ آ آ آ His confidence to equal God in power.
آ آ آ آ Yet soon he healï؟½d; for Spirits that live throughout
آ آ آ آ Vital in every part, not as frail man
آ آ آ آ In Entrailes, Heart or Head, Liver or Reines,
آ آ آ آ Cannot but by annihilating die;
آ آ آ آ Nor in thir liquid texture mortal wound
آ آ آ آ Receive, no more then can the fluid Aire:
آ آ آ آ All Heart they live, all Head, all Eye, all Eare,
آ آ آ آ All Intellect, all Sense, and as they please,
آ آ آ آ They Limb themselves, and colour, shape or size
آ آ آ آ Assume, as likes them best, condense or rare.
آ آ آ آ Mean while in other parts like deeds deservd
آ آ آ آ Memorial, where the might of Gabriel fought,
آ آ آ آ And with fierce Ensignes piercï؟½d the deep array
آ آ آ آ Of Moloc furious King, who him defiï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And at his Chariot wheeles to drag him bound
آ آ آ آ Threatnï؟½d, nor from the Holie One of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Refreinï؟½d his tongue blasphemous; but anon
آ آ آ آ Down clovï؟½n to the waste, with shatterd Armes
آ آ آ آ And uncouth paine fled bellowing. On each wing
آ آ آ آ Uriel and Raphael his vaunting foe,
آ آ آ آ Though huge, and in a Rock of Diamond Armd,
آ آ آ آ Vanquishï؟½d Adramelec, and Asmadai,
آ آ آ آ Two potent Thrones, that to be less then Gods
آ آ آ آ Disdainï؟½d, but meaner thoughts learnd in thir flight,
آ آ آ آ Manglï؟½d with gastly wounds through Plate and Maile.
آ آ آ آ Nor stood unmindful Abdiel to annoy
آ آ آ آ The Atheist crew, but with redoublï؟½d blow
آ آ آ آ Ariel and Arioc, and the violence
آ آ آ آ Of Ramiel scorcht and blasted overthrew.
آ آ آ آ I might relate of thousands, and thir names
آ آ آ آ Eternize here on Earth; but those elect
آ آ آ آ Angels contented with thir fame in Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Seek not the praise of men: the other sort
آ آ آ آ In might though wondrous and in Acts of Warr,
آ آ آ آ Nor of Renown less eager, yet by doome
آ آ آ آ Canceld from Heavï؟½n and sacred memorie,
آ آ آ آ Nameless in dark oblivion let them dwell.
آ آ آ آ For strength from Truth divided and from Just,
آ آ آ آ Illaudable, naught merits but dispraise
آ آ آ آ And ignominie, yet to glorie aspires
آ آ آ آ Vain glorious, and through infamie seeks fame:
آ آ آ آ Therfore Eternal silence be thir doome.
آ آ آ آ And now thir mightiest quelld, the battel swervï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ With many an inrode gorï؟½d; deformed rout
آ آ آ آ Enterï؟½d, and foul disorder; all the ground
آ آ آ آ With shiverd armour strowï؟½n, and on a heap
آ آ آ آ Chariot and Charioter lay overturnd
آ آ آ آ And fierie foaming Steeds; what stood, recoyld
آ آ آ آ Orewearied, through the faint Satanic Host
آ آ آ آ Defensive scarse, or with pale fear surprisï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Then first with fear surprisï؟½d and sense of paine
آ آ آ آ Fled ignominious, to such evil brought
آ آ آ آ By sinne of disobedience, till that hour
آ آ آ آ Not liable to fear or flight or paine.
آ آ آ آ Far otherwise thï؟½ inviolable Saints
آ آ آ آ In Cubic Phalanx firm advancï؟½t entire,
آ آ آ آ Invulnerable, impenitrably armï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Such high advantages thir innocence
آ آ آ آ Gave them above thir foes, not to have sinnd,
آ آ آ آ Not to have disobeiï؟½d; in fight they stood
آ آ آ آ Unwearied, unobnoxious to be painï؟½d
آ آ آ آ By wound, though from thir place by violence movï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Now Night her course began, and over Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Inducing darkness, grateful truce imposï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And silence on the odious dinn of Warr:
آ آ آ آ Under her Cloudie covert both retirï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Victor and Vanquisht: on the foughten field
آ آ آ آ Michael and his Angels prevalent
آ آ آ آ Encamping, placï؟½d in Guard thir Watches round,
آ آ آ آ Cherubic waving fires: on thï؟½ other part
آ آ آ آ Satan with his rebellious disappeerd,
آ آ آ آ Far in the dark dislodgï؟½d, and void of rest,
آ آ آ آ His Potentates to Councel callï؟½d by night;
آ آ آ آ And in the midst thus undismaiï؟½d began.
آ آ آ آ O now in danger triï؟½d, now known in Armes
آ آ آ آ Not to be overpowerd, Companions deare,
آ آ آ آ Found worthy not of Libertie alone,
آ آ آ آ Too mean pretense, but what we more affect,
آ آ آ آ Honour, Dominion, Glorie, and renowne,
آ آ آ آ Who have sustaind one day in doubtful fight,
آ آ آ آ (And if one day, why not Eternal dayes?)
آ آ آ آ What Heavens Lord had powerfullest to send
آ آ آ آ Against us from about his Throne, and judgï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Sufficient to subdue us to his will,
آ آ آ آ But proves not so: then fallible, it seems,
آ آ آ آ Of future we may deem him, though till now
آ آ آ آ Omniscient thought. True is, less firmly armï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Some disadvantage we endurï؟½d and paine,
آ آ آ آ Till now not known, but known as soon contemnd,
آ آ آ آ Since now we find this our Empyreal forme
آ آ آ آ Incapable of mortal injurie
آ آ آ آ Imperishable, and though peircï؟½d with wound,
آ آ آ آ Soon closing, and by native vigour healï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Of evil then so small as easie think
آ آ آ آ The remedie; perhaps more valid Armes,
آ آ آ آ Weapons more violent, when next we meet,
آ آ آ آ May serve to better us, and worse our foes,
آ آ آ آ Or equal what between us made the odds,
آ آ آ آ In Nature none: if other hidden cause
آ آ آ آ Left them Superiour, while we can preserve
آ آ آ آ Unhurt our mindes, and understanding sound,
آ آ آ آ Due search and consultation will disclose.
آ آ آ آ He sat; and in thï؟½ assembly next upstood
آ آ آ آ Nisroc, of Principalities the prime;
آ آ آ آ As one he stood escapï؟½t from cruel fight,
آ آ آ آ Sore toild, his rivï؟½n Armes to havoc hewn,
آ آ آ آ And cloudie in aspect thus answering spake.
آ آ آ آ Deliverer from new Lords, leader to free
آ آ آ آ Enjoyment of our right as Gods; yet hard
آ آ آ آ For Gods, and too unequal work we find
آ آ آ آ Against unequal armes to fight in paine,
آ آ آ آ Against unpaind, impassive; from which evil
آ آ آ آ Ruin must needs ensue; for what availes
آ آ آ آ Valour or strength, though matchless, quelld with pain
آ آ آ آ Which all subdues, and makes remiss the hands
آ آ آ آ Of Mightiest. Sense of pleasure we may well
آ آ آ آ Spare out of life perhaps, and not repine,
آ آ آ آ But live content, which is the calmest life:
آ آ آ آ But pain is perfet miserie, the worst
آ آ آ آ Of evils, and excessive, overturnes
آ آ آ آ All patience. He who therefore can invent
آ آ آ آ With what more forcible we may offend
آ آ آ آ Our yet unwounded Enemies, or arme
آ آ آ آ Our selves with like defence, to me deserves
آ آ آ آ No less then for deliverance what we owe.
آ آ آ آ Whereto with look composï؟½d Satan repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Not uninvented that, which thou aright
آ آ آ آ Beleivst so main to our success, I bring;
آ آ آ آ Which of us who beholds the bright surface
آ آ آ آ Of this Ethereous mould whereon we stand,
آ آ آ آ This continent of spacious Heavï؟½n, adornd
آ آ آ آ With Plant, Fruit, Flour Ambrosial, Gemms and Gold,
آ آ آ آ Whose Eye so superficially surveyes
آ آ آ آ These things, as not to mind from whence they grow
آ آ آ آ Deep under ground, materials dark and crude,
آ آ آ آ Of spiritous and fierie spume, till toucht
آ آ آ آ With Heavï؟½ns ray, and temperd they shoot forth
آ آ آ آ So beauteous, opï؟½ning to the ambient light.
آ آ آ آ These in thir dark Nativitie the Deep
آ آ آ آ Shall yeild us, pregnant with infernal flame,
آ آ آ آ Which into hallow Engins long and round
آ آ آ آ Thick-rammd, at thï؟½ other bore with touch of fire
آ آ آ آ Dilated and infuriate shall send forth
آ آ آ آ From far with thundring noise among our foes
آ آ آ آ Such implements of mischief as shall dash
آ آ آ آ To pieces, and orewhelm whatever stands
آ آ آ آ Adverse, that they shall fear we have disarmd
آ آ آ آ The Thunderer of his only dreaded bolt.
آ آ آ آ Nor long shall be our labour, yet ere dawne,
آ آ آ آ Effect shall end our wish. Mean while revive;
آ آ آ آ Abandon fear; to strength and counsel joind
آ آ آ آ Think nothing hard, much less to be despaird.
آ آ آ آ He ended, and his words thir drooping chere
آ آ آ آ Enlightnï؟½d, and thir languisht hope revivï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ invention all admirï؟½d, and each, how hee
آ آ آ آ To be thï؟½ inventer missï؟½d, so easie it seemd
آ آ آ آ Once found, which yet unfound most would have thought
آ آ آ آ Impossible: yet haply of thy Race
آ آ آ آ In future dayes, if Malice should abound,
آ آ آ آ Some one intent on mischief, or inspirï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With devï؟½lish machination might devise
آ آ آ آ Like instrument to plague the Sons of men
آ آ آ آ For sin, on warr and mutual slaughter bent.
آ آ آ آ Forthwith from Councel to the work they flew,
آ آ آ آ None arguing stood, innumerable hands
آ آ آ آ Were ready, in a moment up they turnd
آ آ آ آ Wide the Celestial soile, and saw beneath
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ originals of Nature in thir crude
آ آ آ آ Conception; Sulphurous and Nitrous Foame
آ آ آ آ They found, they minglï؟½d, and with suttle Art,
آ آ آ آ Concocted and adusted they reducï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To blackest grain, and into store conveyd:
آ آ آ آ Part hiddï؟½n veins diggd up (nor hath this Earth
آ آ آ آ Entrails unlike) of Mineral and Stone,
آ آ آ آ Whereof to found thir Engins and thir Balls
آ آ آ آ Of missive ruin; part incentive reed
آ آ آ آ Provide, pernicious with one touch to fire.
آ آ آ آ So all ere day spring, under conscious Night
آ آ آ آ Secret they finishï؟½d, and in order set,
آ آ آ آ With silent circumspection unespiï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Now when fair Morn Orient in Heavï؟½n appeerd
آ آ آ آ Up rose the Victor Angels, and to Arms
آ آ آ آ The matin Trumpet Sung: in Arms they stood
آ آ آ آ Of Golden Panoplie, refulgent Host,
آ آ آ آ Soon banded; others from the dawning Hills
آ آ آ آ Lookd round, and Scouts each Coast light-armed scoure,
آ آ آ آ Each quarter, to descrie the distant foe,
آ آ آ آ Where lodgï؟½d, or whither fled, or if for fight,
آ آ آ آ In motion or in alt: him soon they met
آ آ آ آ Under spred Ensignes moving nigh, in slow
آ آ آ آ But firm Battalion; back with speediest Sail
آ آ آ آ Zephiel, of Cherubim the swiftest wing,
آ آ آ آ Came flying, and in mid Aire aloud thus criï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Arme, Warriours, Arme for fight, the foe at hand,
آ آ آ آ Whom fled we thought, will save us long pursuit
آ آ آ آ This day, fear not his flight; so thick a Cloud
آ آ آ آ He comes, and settlï؟½d in his face I see
آ آ آ آ Sad resolution and secure: let each
آ آ آ آ His Adamantine coat gird well, and each
آ آ آ آ Fit well his Helme, gripe fast his orbed Shield,
آ آ آ آ Born eevn or high, for this day will pour down,
آ آ آ آ If I conjecture aught, no drizling showr,
آ آ آ آ But ratling storm of Arrows barbd with fire.
آ آ آ آ So warnd he them aware themselves, and soon
آ آ آ آ In order, quit of all impediment;
آ آ آ آ Instant without disturb they took Allarm,
آ آ آ آ And onward move Embattelld; when behold
آ آ آ آ Not distant far with heavie pace the Foe
آ آ آ آ Approaching gross and huge; in hollow Cube
آ آ آ آ Training his devilish Enginrie, impalï؟½d
آ آ آ آ On every side with shaddowing Squadrons Deep,
آ آ آ آ To hide the fraud. At interview both stood
آ آ آ آ A while, but suddenly at head appeerd
آ آ آ آ Satan: And thus was heard Commanding loud.
آ آ آ آ Vangard, to Right and Left the Front unfould;
آ آ آ آ That all may see who hate us, how we seek
آ آ آ آ Peace and composure, and with open brest
آ آ آ آ Stand readie to receive them, if they like
آ آ آ آ Our overture, and turn not back perverse;
آ آ آ آ But that I doubt, however witness Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Heavï؟½n witness thou anon, while we discharge
آ آ آ آ Freely our part: yee who appointed stand
آ آ آ آ Do as you have in charge, and briefly touch
آ آ آ آ What we propound, and loud that all may hear.
آ آ آ آ So scoffing in ambiguous words, he scarce
آ آ آ آ Had ended; when to Right and Left the Front
آ آ آ آ Divided, and to either Flank retirï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Which to our eyes discoverd new and strange,
آ آ آ آ A triple-mounted row of Pillars laid
آ آ آ آ On Wheels (for like to Pillars most they seemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Or hollowï؟½d bodies made of Oak or Firr
آ آ آ آ With branches lopt, in Wood or Mountain fellï؟½d)
آ آ آ آ Brass, Iron, Stonie mould, had not thir mouthes
آ آ آ آ With hideous orifice gapï؟½t on us wide,
آ آ آ آ Portending hollow truce; at each behind
آ آ آ آ A Seraph stood, and in his hand a Reed
آ آ آ آ Stood waving tipt with fire; while we suspense,
آ آ آ آ Collected stood within our thoughts amusï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Not long, for sudden all at once thir Reeds
آ آ آ آ Put forth, and to a narrow vent appliï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With nicest touch. Immediate in a flame,
آ آ آ آ But soon obscurï؟½d with smoak, all Heavï؟½n appeerd,
آ آ آ آ From those deep-throated Engins belcht, whose roar
آ آ آ آ Emboweld with outragious noise the Air,
آ آ آ آ And all her entrails tore, disgorging foule
آ آ آ آ Thir devillish glut, chaind Thunderbolts and Hail
آ آ آ آ Of Iron Globes, which on the Victor Host
آ آ آ آ Levelï؟½d, with such impetuous furie smote,
آ آ آ آ That whom they hit, none on thir feet might stand,
آ آ آ آ Though standing else as Rocks, but down they fell
آ آ آ آ By thousands, Angel on Arch-Angel rowlï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ The sooner for thir Arms, unarmï؟½d they might
آ آ آ آ Have easily as Spirits evaded swift
آ آ آ آ By quick contraction or remove; but now
آ آ آ آ Foule dissipation followï؟½d and forcï؟½t rout;
آ آ آ آ Nor servï؟½d it to relax thir serried files.
آ آ آ آ What should they do? if on they rusht, repulse
آ آ آ آ Repeated, and indecent overthrow
آ آ آ آ Doublï؟½d, would render them yet more despisï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And to thir foes a laughter; for in view
آ آ آ آ Stood rankt of Seraphim another row
آ آ آ آ In posture to displode thir second tire
آ آ آ آ Of Thunder: back defeated to return
آ آ آ آ They worse abhorrï؟½d. Satan beheld thir plight,
آ آ آ آ And to his Mates thus in derision callï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ O Friends, why come not on these Victors proud?
آ آ آ آ Ere while they fierce were coming, and when wee,
آ آ آ آ To entertain them fair with open Front
آ آ آ آ And Brest, (what could we more?) propounded terms
آ آ آ آ Of composition, strait they changï؟½d thir minds,
آ آ آ آ Flew off, and into strange vagaries fell,
آ آ آ آ As they would dance, yet for a dance they seemd
آ آ آ آ Somwhat extravagant and wilde, perhaps
آ آ آ آ For joy of offerd peace: but I suppose
آ آ آ آ If our proposals once again were heard
آ آ آ آ We should compel them to a quick result.
آ آ آ آ To whom thus Belial in like gamesom mood.
آ آ آ آ Leader, the terms we sent were terms of weight,
آ آ آ آ Of hard contents, and full of force urgï؟½d home,
آ آ آ آ Such as we might perceive amusï؟½d them all,
آ آ آ آ And stumblï؟½d many, who receives them right,
آ آ آ آ Had need from head to foot well understand;
آ آ آ آ Not understood, this gift they have besides,
آ آ آ آ They shew us when our foes walk not upright.
آ آ آ آ So they among themselves in pleasant veine
آ آ آ آ Stood scoffing, highthnï؟½d in thir thoughts beyond
آ آ آ آ All doubt of Victorie, eternal might
آ آ آ آ To match with thir inventions they presumï؟½d
آ آ آ آ So easie, and of his Thunder made a scorn,
آ آ آ آ And all his Host derided, while they stood
آ آ آ آ A while in trouble; but they stood not long,
آ آ آ آ Rage prompted them at length, and found them arms
آ آ آ آ Against such hellish mischief fit to oppose.
آ آ آ آ Forthwith (behold the excellence, the power
آ آ آ آ Which God hath in his mighty Angels placï؟½d)
آ آ آ آ Thir Arms away they threw, and to the Hills
آ آ آ آ (For Earth hath this variety from Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Of pleasure situate in Hill and Dale)
آ آ آ آ Light as the Lightning glimps they ran, they flew,
آ آ آ آ From thir foundations loosning to and fro
آ آ آ آ They pluckt the seated Hills with all thir load,
آ آ آ آ Rocks, Waters, Woods, and by the shaggie tops
آ آ آ آ Up lifting bore them in thir hands: Amaze,
آ آ آ آ Be sure, and terrour seisï؟½d the rebel Host,
آ آ آ آ When coming towards them so dread they saw
آ آ آ آ The bottom of the Mountains upward turnï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Till on those cursed Engins triple-row
آ آ آ آ They saw them whelmd, and all thir confidence
آ آ آ آ Under the weight of Mountains buried deep,
آ آ آ آ Themselves invaded next, and on thir heads
آ آ آ آ Main Promontories flung, which in the Air
آ آ آ آ Came shadowing, and opprest whole Legions armï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Thir armor helpï؟½d thir harm, crushï؟½t in and brusï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Into thir substance pent, which wrought them pain
آ آ آ آ Implacable, and many a dolorous groan,
آ آ آ آ Long strugling underneath, ere they could wind
آ آ آ آ Out of such prison, though Spirits of purest light,
آ آ آ آ Purest at first, now gross by sinning grown.
آ آ آ آ The rest in imitation to like Armes
آ آ آ آ Betook them, and the neighbouring Hills uptore;
آ آ آ آ So Hills amid the Air encounterd Hills
آ آ آ آ Hurlï؟½d to and fro with jaculation dire,
آ آ آ آ That under ground they fought in dismal shade;
آ آ آ آ Infernal noise; Warr seemï؟½d a civil Game
آ آ آ آ To this uproar; horrid confusion heapt
آ آ آ آ Upon confusion rose: and now all Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Had gone to wrack, with ruin overspred,
آ آ آ آ Had not thï؟½ Almightie Father where he sits
آ آ آ آ Shrinï؟½d in his Sanctuarie of Heavï؟½n secure,
آ آ آ آ Consulting on the sum of things, foreseen
آ آ آ آ This tumult, and permitted all, advisï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ That his great purpose he might so fulfill,
آ آ آ آ To honour his Anointed Son avengï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Upon his enemies, and to declare
آ آ آ آ All power on him transferrï؟½d: whence to his Son
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Assessor of his Throne he thus began.
آ آ آ آ Effulgence of my Glorie, Son belovï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Son in whose face invisible is beheld
آ آ آ آ Visibly, what by Deitie I am,
آ آ آ آ And in whose hand what by Decree I doe,
آ آ آ آ Second Omnipotence, two dayes are past,
آ آ آ آ Two dayes, as we compute the dayes of Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ Since Michael and his Powers went forth to tame
آ آ آ آ These disobedient; sore hath been thir fight,
آ آ آ آ As likeliest was, when two such Foes met armï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ For to themselves I left them, and thou knowst,
آ آ آ آ Equal in their Creation they were formï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Save what sin hath impaird, which yet hath wrought
آ آ آ آ Insensibly, for I suspend thir doom;
آ آ آ آ Whence in perpetual fight they needs must last
آ آ آ آ Endless, and no solution will be found:
آ آ آ آ Warr wearied hath performï؟½d what Warr can do,
آ آ آ آ And to disorderï؟½d rage let loose the reines,
آ آ آ آ With Mountains as with Weapons armï؟½d, which makes
آ آ آ آ Wild work in Heavï؟½n, and dangerous to the maine.
آ آ آ آ Two dayes are therefore past, the third is thine;
آ آ آ آ For thee I have ordainï؟½d it, and thus farr
آ آ آ آ Have sufferd, that the Glorie may be thine
آ آ آ آ Of ending this great Warr, since none but Thou
آ آ آ آ Can end it. Into thee such Vertue and Grace
آ آ آ آ Immense I have transfusï؟½d, that all may know
آ آ آ آ In Heavï؟½n and Hell thy Power above compare,
آ آ آ آ And this perverse Commotion governd thus,
آ آ آ آ To manifest thee worthiest to be Heir
آ آ آ آ Of all things, to be Heir and to be King
آ آ آ آ By Sacred Unction, thy deserved right.
آ آ آ آ Go then thou Mightiest in thy Fathers might,
آ آ آ آ Ascend my Chariot, guide the rapid Wheeles
آ آ آ آ That shake Heavï؟½ns basis, bring forth all my Warr,
آ آ آ آ My Bow and Thunder, my Almightie Arms
آ آ آ آ Gird on, and Sword upon thy puissant Thigh;
آ آ آ آ Pursue these sons of Darkness, drive them out
آ آ آ آ From all Heavï؟½ns bounds into the utter Deep:
آ آ آ آ There let them learn, as likes them, to despise
آ آ آ آ God and Messiah his anointed King.
آ آ آ آ He said, and on his Son with Rayes direct
آ آ آ آ Shon full, he all his Father full exprest
آ آ آ آ Ineffably into his face receivï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And thus the filial Godhead answering spake.
آ آ آ آ O Father, O Supream of heavï؟½nly Thrones,
آ آ آ آ First, Highest, Holiest, Best, thou alwayes seekst
آ آ آ آ To glorifie thy Son, I alwayes thee,
آ آ آ آ As is most just; this I my Glorie account,
آ آ آ آ My exaltation, and my whole delight,
آ آ آ آ That thou in me well pleasï؟½d, declarst thy will
آ آ آ آ Fulfillï؟½d, which to fulfil is all my bliss.
آ آ آ آ Scepter and Power, thy giving, I assume,
آ آ آ آ And gladlier shall resign, when in the end
آ آ آ آ Thou shalt be All in All, and I in thee
آ آ آ آ For ever, and in mee all whom thou lovï؟½st:
آ آ آ آ But whom thou hatï؟½st, I hate, and can put on
آ آ آ آ Thy terrors, as I put thy mildness on,
آ آ آ آ Image of thee in all things; and shall soon,
آ آ آ آ Armd with thy might, rid heavï؟½n of these rebellï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ To thir preparï؟½d ill Mansion driven down
آ آ آ آ To chains of Darkness, and thï؟½ undying Worm,
آ آ آ آ That from thy just obedience could revolt,
آ آ آ آ Whom to obey is happiness entire.
آ آ آ آ Then shall thy Saints unmixt, and from thï؟½ impure
آ آ آ آ Farr separate, circling thy holy Mount
آ آ آ آ Unfained Halleluiahs to thee sing,
آ آ آ آ Hymns of high praise, and I among them chief.
آ آ آ آ So said, he oï؟½re his Scepter bowing, rose
آ آ آ آ From the right hand of Glorie where he sate,
آ آ آ آ And the third sacred Morn began to shine
آ آ آ آ Dawning through Heavï؟½n: forth rushï؟½d with whirlwind sound
آ آ آ آ The Chariot of Paternal Deitie,
آ آ آ آ Flashing thick flames, Wheele within Wheele undrawn,
آ آ آ آ It self instinct with Spirit, but convoyd
آ آ آ آ By four Cherubic shapes, four Faces each
آ آ آ آ Had wondrous, as with Starrs thir bodies all
آ آ آ آ And Wings were set with Eyes, with Eyes the Wheels
آ آ آ آ Of Beril, and careering Fires between;
آ آ آ آ Over thir heads a chrystal Firmament,
آ آ آ آ Whereon a Saphir Throne, inlaid with pure
آ آ آ آ Amber, and colours of the showrie Arch.
آ آ آ آ Hee in Celestial Panoplie all armd
آ آ آ آ Of radiant Urim, work divinely wrought,
آ آ آ آ Ascended, at his right hand Victorie
آ آ آ آ Sate Eagle-wingï؟½d, beside him hung his Bow
آ آ آ آ And Quiver with three-bolted Thunder storï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And from about him fierce Effusion rowld
آ آ آ آ Of smoak and bickering flame, and sparkles dire;
آ آ آ آ Attended with ten thousand thousand Saints,
آ آ آ آ He onward came, farr off his coming shon,
آ آ آ آ And twentie thousand (I thir number heard)
آ آ آ آ Chariots of God, half on each hand were seen:
آ آ آ آ Hee on the wings of Cherub rode sublime
آ آ آ آ On the Crystallin Skie, in Saphir Thronï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Illustrious farr and wide, but by his own
آ آ آ آ First seen, them unexpected joy surprizï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ When the great Ensign of Messiah blazï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Aloft by Angels born, his Sign in Heavï؟½n:
آ آ آ آ Under whose Conduct Michael soon reducï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His Armie, circumfusï؟½d on either Wing,
آ آ آ آ Under thir Head imbodied all in one.
آ آ آ آ Before him Power Divine his way preparï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ At his command the uprooted Hills retirï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Each to his place, they heard his voice and went
آ آ آ آ Obsequious, Heavï؟½n his wonted face renewd,
آ آ آ آ And with fresh Flourets Hill and Valley smilï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ This saw his hapless Foes, but stood obdurï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And to rebellious fight rallied thir Powers
آ آ آ آ Insensate, hope conceiving from despair.
آ آ آ آ In heavï؟½nly Spirits could such perverseness dwell?
آ آ آ آ But to convince the proud what Signs availe,
آ آ آ آ Or Wonders move thï؟½ obdurate to relent?
آ آ آ آ They hardï؟½nd more by what might most reclame,
آ آ آ آ Grieving to see his Glorie, at the sight
آ آ آ آ Took envie, and aspiring to his highth,
آ آ آ آ Stood reimbattellï؟½d fierce, by force or fraud
آ آ آ آ Weening to prosper, and at length prevaile
آ آ آ آ Against God and Messiah, or to fall
آ آ آ آ In universal ruin last, and now
آ آ آ آ To final Battel drew, disdaining flight,
آ آ آ آ Or faint retreat; when the great Son of God
آ آ آ آ To all his Host on either hand thus spake.
آ آ آ آ Stand still in bright array ye Saints, here stand
آ آ آ آ Ye Angels armï؟½d, this day from Battel rest;
آ آ آ آ Faithful hath been your Warfare, and of God
آ آ آ آ Accepted, fearless in his righteous Cause,
آ آ آ آ And as ye have receivd, so have ye don
آ آ آ آ Invincibly; but of this cursed crew
آ آ آ آ The punishment to other hand belongs,
آ آ آ آ Vengeance is his, or whose he sole appoints;
آ آ آ آ Number to this dayes work is not ordainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Nor multitude, stand onely and behold
آ آ آ آ Gods indignation on these Godless pourd
آ آ آ آ By mee; not you but mee they have despisï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Yet envied; against mee is all thir rage,
آ آ آ آ Because the Father, tï؟½ whom in Heavï؟½n supream
آ آ آ آ Kingdom and Power and Glorie appertains,
آ آ آ آ Hath honourd me according to his will.
آ آ آ آ Therefore to mee thir doom he hath assigï؟½nï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ That they may have thir wish, to trie with mee
آ آ آ آ In Battel which the stronger proves, they all,
آ آ آ آ Or I alone against them, since by strength
آ آ آ آ They measure all, of other excellence
آ آ آ آ Not emulous, nor care who them excells;
آ آ آ آ Nor other strife with them do I voutsafe.
آ آ آ آ So spake the Son, and into terrour changï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His countï؟½nance too severe to be beheld
آ آ آ آ And full of wrauth bent on his Enemies.
آ آ آ آ At once the Four spred out thir Starrie wings
آ آ آ آ With dreadful shade contiguous, and the Orbes
آ آ آ آ Of his fierce Chariot rowld, as with the sound
آ آ آ آ Of torrent Floods, or of a numerous Host.
آ آ آ آ Hee on his impious Foes right onward drove,
آ آ آ آ Gloomie as Night; under his burning Wheeles
آ آ آ آ The stedfast Empyrean shook throughout,
آ آ آ آ All but the Throne it self of God. Full soon
آ آ آ آ Among them he arrivï؟½d; in his right hand
آ آ آ آ Grasping ten thousand Thunders, which he sent
آ آ آ آ Before him, such as in thir Soules infixï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Plagues; they astonisht all resistance lost,
آ آ آ آ All courage; down thir idle weapons dropï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½re Shields and Helmes, and helmed heads he rode
آ آ آ آ Of Thrones and mighty Seraphim prostrate,
آ آ آ آ That wishï؟½d the Mountains now might be again
آ آ آ آ Thrown on them as a shelter from his ire.
آ آ آ آ Nor less on either side tempestuous fell
آ آ آ آ His arrows, from the fourfold-visagï؟½d Foure,
آ آ آ آ Distinct with eyes, and from the living Wheels,
آ آ آ آ Distinct alike with multitude of eyes,
آ آ آ آ One Spirit in them rulï؟½d, and every eye
آ آ آ آ Glarï؟½d lightning, and shot forth pernicious fire
آ آ آ آ Among thï؟½ accurst, that witherd all thir strength,
آ آ آ آ And of thir wonted vigour left them draind,
آ آ آ آ Exhausted, spiritless, afflicted, fallï؟½n.
آ آ آ آ Yet half his strength he put not forth, but checkï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His Thunder in mid Volie, for he meant
آ آ آ آ Not to destroy, but root them out of Heavï؟½n:
آ آ آ آ The overthrown he raisï؟½d, and as a Heard
آ آ آ آ Of Goats or timerous flock together throngd
آ آ آ آ Drove them before him Thunder-struck, pursuï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With terrors and with furies to the bounds
آ آ آ آ And Chrystall wall of Heavï؟½n, which opï؟½ning wide,
آ آ آ آ Rowld inward, and a spacious Gap disclosï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Into the wastful Deep; the monstrous sight
آ آ آ آ Strook them with horror backward, but far worse
آ آ آ آ Urgï؟½d them behind; headlong themselvs they threw
آ آ آ آ Down from the verge of Heavï؟½n, Eternal wrauth
آ آ آ آ Burnt after them to the bottomless pit.
آ آ آ آ Hell heard thï؟½ unsufferable noise, Hell saw
آ آ آ آ Heavï؟½n ruining from Heavï؟½n and would have fled
آ آ آ آ Affrighted; but strict Fate had cast too deep
آ آ آ آ Her dark foundations, and too fast had bound.
آ آ آ آ Nine dayes they fell; confounded Chaos roard,
آ آ آ آ And felt tenfold confusion in thir fall
آ آ آ آ Through his wilde Anarchie, so huge a rout
آ آ آ آ Incumberd him with ruin: Hell at last
آ آ آ آ Yawning receavd them whole, and on them closï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Hell thir fit habitation fraught with fire
آ آ آ آ Unquenchable, the house of woe and paine.
آ آ آ آ Disburdï؟½nd Heavï؟½n rejoicï؟½d, and soon repaird
آ آ آ آ Her mural breach, returning whence it rowld.
آ آ آ آ Sole Victor from thï؟½ expulsion of his Foes
آ آ آ آ Messiah his triumphal Chariot turnd:
آ آ آ آ To meet him all his Saints, who silent stood
آ آ آ آ Eye witnesses of his Almightie Acts,
آ آ آ آ With Jubilie advancï؟½d; and as they went,
آ آ آ آ Shaded with branching Palme, each order bright,
آ آ آ آ Sung Triumph, and him sung Victorious King,
آ آ آ آ Son, Heire, and Lord, to him Dominion givï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ Worthiest to Reign: he celebrated rode
آ آ آ آ Triumphant through mid Heavï؟½n, into the Courts
آ آ آ آ And Temple of his mightie Father Thronï؟½d
آ آ آ آ On high; who into Glorie him receavï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Where now he sits at the right hand of bliss.
آ آ آ آ Thus measuring things in Heavï؟½n by things on Earth
آ آ آ آ At thy request, and that thou maist beware
آ آ آ آ By what is past, to thee I have revealï؟½d
آ آ آ آ What might have else to human Race bin hid;
آ آ آ آ The discord which befel, and Warr in Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Among thï؟½ Angelic Powers, and the deep fall
آ آ آ آ Of those too high aspiring, who rebelld
آ آ آ آ With Satan, hee who envies now thy state,
آ آ آ آ Who now is plotting how he may seduce
آ آ آ آ Thee also from obedience, that with him
آ آ آ آ Bereavd of happiness thou maist partake
آ آ آ آ His punishment, Eternal miserie;
آ آ آ آ Which would be all his solace and revenge,
آ آ آ آ As a despite don against the most High,
آ آ آ آ Thee once to gaine Companion of his woe.
آ آ آ آ But listï؟½n not to his Temptations, warne
آ آ آ آ Thy weaker; let it profit thee to have heard
آ آ آ آ By terrible Example the reward
آ آ آ آ Of disobedience; firm they might have stood,
آ آ آ آ Yet fell; remember, and fear to transgress.
meaning:
"Paradise Lost - Book VI" is a quintessential example of John Milton's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
